Alex Morgan: Welcome to The Long View. Today we're exploring continuous glucose monitors and what they tell you in depth with Dr. Elena Fischer, researcher in metabolic and longevity science.
Dr. Elena Fischer: Thanks, Alex Morgan. This is a topic I've spent decades on.
Alex Morgan: This episode is brought to you by Linear. Linear is the issue tracker built for speed — keyboard-first, fast search, and a clean roadmap view your team actually uses. Get started at linear.com/podcast.Why is systems thinking the part that most analyses get wrong?
Dr. Elena Fischer: The short answer is CGMs are diagnostic tools more than they are training tools. Teams working with WHOOP run into this constantly. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Alex Morgan: Is there a counterexample that would change your view?
Dr. Elena Fischer: That ties to another point — I changed my mind on CGM use for general population — turns out 80% gain at 5% of intervention cost is wellness, not biohacking. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Alex Morgan: Let's talk about risk management. What's your read?
Dr. Elena Fischer: Honestly, Most postprandial glucose 'spikes' are noise to anyone without metabolic dysfunction. Teams working with Levels run into this constantly. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
Alex Morgan: What would you tell the version of you from five years ago?
Dr. Elena Fischer: That ties to another point — CGMs are diagnostic tools more than they are training tools. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
Alex Morgan: What's the deepest piece of context on reliability that listeners are missing?
Dr. Elena Fischer: I'd put it this way: The systems-thinking version of CGM use is feedback loops, not gamification. Teams working with InsideTracker run into this constantly. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Alex Morgan: Say more about that.
Dr. Elena Fischer: That ties to another point — Most postprandial glucose 'spikes' are noise to anyone without metabolic dysfunction. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Alex Morgan: Walk me through how reliability interacts with the broader continuous glucose monitors and what they tell you story.
Dr. Elena Fischer: Honestly, I changed my mind on CGM use for general population — turns out 80% gain at 5% of intervention cost is wellness, not biohacking. Teams working with InsideTracker run into this constantly. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
Alex Morgan: That tracks.
Dr. Elena Fischer: That ties to another point — The systems-thinking version of CGM use is feedback loops, not gamification. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
